title: Jacksonville's first known Jewish family arrives
body: The Dzialynski family arrives from Prussian Poland — the city's first known Jews. By 1857 the community establishes a Jewish section in the Old City Cemetery, Florida's first permanent Jewish organization.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: Congregation Ahavath Chesed chartered
body: Jacksonville's first congregation (and Florida's second) is chartered; it affiliates Reform in the 1890s — prompting traditionalist families to organize on their own.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: Hebrew Orthodox Congregation B'nai Israel incorporated
body: Five Orthodox families, by then numbering about forty families, formally organize B'nai Israel. The charter was approved on December 5 and recorded the next day, December 6. Max Frank is named first president; the incorporators include Samuel Controvitz, E. H. Pilton, Morris Wexler, A. Hirsch, Alex Ossinsky, and Louis Rosenstein. The Center's anniversary timeline identifies many of the families as immigrants from Pušalotas, Lithuania.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: A synagogue in LaVilla
body: With membership at seventy-five, B'nai Israel begins building at Jefferson and Duval Streets in LaVilla. The 1907 building campaign and a 1909 completion/dedication describe successive stages of the same project, rather than competing dates.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: The YMHA is founded
body: The Young Men's Hebrew Association organizes in LaVilla. By 1926-27 the first yearbook is arguing forcefully that 'an adequate Y building be erected in this City.'

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: The Conservative turn — Rabbi Samuel Benjamin elected
body: On April 1, 1926, the congregation elects Rabbi Samuel Benjamin, a Jewish Theological Seminary graduate. He brings the 'synagogue-center' vision: one roof for worship, school, gym, and social life. The religious school is reorganized in May 1926 and registers 125 pupils, running straight through the summer.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: A new Center, and a surviving Year Book
body: The congregation purchases land at Third and Silver Streets and adopts the Jacksonville Jewish Center identity. Its 1926-27 (5687) volume calls itself a 'first attempt to produce a Year Book.' The 125th-anniversary timeline adds an important qualification: earlier editions dating to 1902 were largely congregational directories, while it identifies 1935 as the first yearbook in the form recognized today.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Dedication at Third & Silver
body: The new Center building in Springfield is dedicated with a full program: Rabbi Samuel Benjamin, Cantor Aaron I. Edgar, the YMHA Orchestra, greetings from every Jewish organization in town, and the presentation of the key from Lionel D. Joel to president Harry Finkelstein. The congregation formally becomes the Jacksonville Jewish Center.

title: The missing years
body: No yearbooks survive for 1931-1933 — the depths of the Great Depression, when Jacksonville Jewish institutions struggled (a sister congregation lost ~140 dues-paying families in 1930). Whether the books were never published or simply never digitized is an open question.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: Ten years in the building
body: The 1938-39 yearbook marks 'the tenth anniversary of this institution' with a banquet at the Seminole Hotel, guest speakers, and — in the same pages — anxious attention to events in Europe.

title: River Garden Hebrew Home founded
body: The community establishes River Garden Hebrew Home for the Aged on the St. Johns River; the Center's yearbooks report on it faithfully for the next seventy years — from an antebellum mansion housing 10 residents to a modern complex serving 175.

title: Rabbi Sanders A. Tofield arrives
body: A scholar prominent in the Conservative movement's Rabbinical Assembly, Tofield leads the Center until his death in 1960.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)
Jacksonville Jewish Center — U.S. National Park Service

title: The first Bat Mitzvah ceremony
body: On April 9, Bryna Lee Datz and Judy Herschaft participate in the Center's first Bat Mitzvah ceremony, held during a late Friday-evening program — an early milestone in women's participation in congregational life.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: The community-center building rises
body: A new community center building (1950) and later classroom annex give the Springfield campus 19 classrooms, chapel, library, social hall, auditorium/gymnasium, banquet facility — even a bridal lounge.

research.title: Jacksonville Jewish Center — U.S. National Park Service

title: National recognition for youth work
body: On May 19, 1952 the Center receives the National United Synagogue Solomon Schechter Award for its youth program — the rabbi's acceptance address fills the yearbook's opening pages, and a Youth Center is dedicated the same year.

title: Bombed on Monday — groundbreaking on Friday
body: On April 28, 1958, dynamite set by 'unapprehended individuals' damages the synagogue; the James Weldon Johnson school is bombed the same night. Four days later, on May 2, the congregation goes ahead with groundbreaking ceremonies for its new school building, photographing the damage and the shovels side by side. 'The Council gave the community the type of leadership and solidarity needed in such a crisis… a pattern for the nation.'

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: The Foundation School opens
body: The Center opens the Foundation School with one first-grade class. In 1966 it is renamed the Solomon Schechter Day School; the 1965-66 yearbook documents the expansion through fifth grade. This corrects the archive's earlier use of the first available yearbook evidence as a founding date.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Beth Shalom splits off
body: Thirteen families leave to found Beth Shalom Congregation, seeking more egalitarian practice. Nearly forty years later, in 2011, the two congregations re-merge.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: Rabbi David Gaffney begins a 29-year rabbinate
body: Rabbi David Gaffney joins the Center, beginning its longest rabbinate. Across twenty-nine years, he leads an expansion of early-childhood, Hebrew high-school, day-school, youth, adult-education, and cultural offerings.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Groundbreaking for the Mandarin campus
body: On September 16, the Center breaks ground for the new synagogue complex. In 1976, a procession carries the Torah scrolls from Third and Silver to the new Mandarin home.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: The move to Mandarin
body: '…whether at 3rd & Silver Streets or 10101 San Jose Blvd., it is the congregation… it is as it has always been.' After a decade of planning and a building campaign, the Center moves to its present campus at San Jose Blvd. and Crown Point Road. The old Springfield building later houses a Job Corps center (1979-2005).

research.title: Jacksonville Jewish Center — U.S. National Park Service

title: Rabbi Dov Kentof joins the Center
body: Rabbi Dov Kentof is hired as Youth and Education Director. The 1999-2000 yearbook, dedicated to him, confirms two decades of service; the anniversary timeline corrects the previous 1980 arrival date.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Ami Shamir's Ark is installed
body: A major building expansion includes the installation of the Ami Shamir-designed Ark in the sanctuary.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: A woman leads the Center
body: Marsha Pollock becomes the first woman in the Center's published presidential succession (1988-1990).

title: Performing Artists Series begins
body: Debbie Friedman inaugurates the Jacksonville Jewish Center's Performing Artists Series, beginning a decade-plus program of Jewish music, culture, and artistry.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Rabbi Kentof retires; a school endowment begins
body: Rabbi Dov Kentof retires after two decades of youth and education work. The Esther Galinsky bequest also establishes the foundation for the Galinsky Academy's educational programs.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Centennial
body: The 2001-02 yearbook celebrates 100 years: 'Jacksonville Jewish Center Centennial Celebration 1901-2001.' That June, the congregation also honors Rabbi Gaffney as he steps down after twenty-nine years and establishes the Rabbi David Gaffney Leadership in Education Award.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Rabbi Jonathan Lubliner arrives
body: Rabbi Jonathan Lubliner becomes the Center's spiritual leader, beginning a period of renewed energy, scholarship, and community engagement.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: The Martin J. Gottlieb Day School
body: The day school is renamed for Martin J. Gottlieb; in 2012 the Center's schools unite under the Galinsky Academy umbrella — DuBow Preschool, Gottlieb Day School, Selevan Religious School, and Setzer Youth Education.

title: The Center adopts egalitarian practice
body: After a fall 2006 study process, the Board approves egalitarian practice in January. At the February 17 inaugural service, Tania Nogelwell leads Shacharit and Lois Tompkins davens Musaf and is counted in the minyan.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Fire in Springfield; a family reunited
body: Fire damages the long-vacant original building at Third & Silver (later demolished). The same year, Beth Shalom Congregation re-merges with the Center and brings a Holocaust Torah — a lasting symbol of remembrance and continuity.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

research.title: ISJL — Jacksonville, Florida Encyclopedia (Goldring/Woldenberg Institute of Southern Jewish Life)

title: The Center's first female rabbi
body: Assistant Rabbi Shira Rosenblum joins the Center as its first female rabbi.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: The preschool is renamed for Laurie DuBow
body: The Center celebrates 75 years of its preschool program. Previously renamed DuBow Preschool, it is renamed Laurie Preschool in 2024 to honor Laurie DuBow, former congregation president and family patriarch.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: Livestream worship expands access
body: During the COVID-19 epidemic, the Center installs a livestream system that enables members to join services and lifecycle moments from home; the system remains an accessibility and connection tool beyond the pandemic.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: On the National Register
body: The Springfield Jacksonville Jewish Center site is added to the National Register of Historic Places, recognizing its significance to Jacksonville's Jewish community.

research.title: Jacksonville Jewish Center — U.S. National Park Service

title: Nashuvah High Holiday service begins
body: The Center introduces Nashuvah, an alternative High Holiday service offered during Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur alongside the sanctuary service.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.

title: 125 years
body: On January 31, 2026, the Jacksonville Jewish Center celebrates its 125th anniversary — an occasion honoring generations of faith, resilience, learning, connection, and communal responsibility.

research.title: JJC 125th Anniversary History Timeline (2026)
research.note: Institutional anniversary brochure; photographs supplied to this project on July 19, 2026.
